title: The Commandments of God for Holy Living: Leviticus 19:1-18
speaker: Elder Scott Hollander
source_files: 10-08-23 -AM- The Commandments of God for Holy Living Leviticus 19_1-18 - Elder Scott Hollander.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Why Leviticus 19?
subpoints: Connects naturally to both the WLC Ten Commandments series and Pastor Lyro's 1 Peter series
1 Peter 1:15-16 quotes Leviticus 19:2 — 'Be holy, for I am holy'
The motto of Leviticus: 'You must be holy for I, the Lord your God, am holy' (Lev 11:44-45; 19:2; 20:7)
Goal of the sermon: (1) define holiness, (2) trace the justification for holy living, (3) apply the commandments to holy living
Methodological goal: train the congregation to be thoughtful readers of Scripture who observe the distinction between Yahweh and Adonai

outline.title: Defining Holiness
subpoints: Holy = utterly distinct, set apart
Yahweh is utterly distinct because he is the sole creator — the only everlastingly existent being; all other powerful beings (angels, demons) are within the creation order
When Yahweh calls his people to be holy as he is holy, he calls them to be set apart from the surrounding nations
The communicable attributes in which his people share: righteousness, justice, love, mercy, grace, kindness (fruit of the Spirit, Gal 5:22-23)

outline.title: Point 1: The Justification for Holy Living
subpoints: Justification 1: Yahweh is holy (v. 2) — God commands holiness from his people because he is holy; 'for' indicates reason
Justification 2: The name Yahweh — the phrase 'I am Yahweh your God' repeated seven times in vv. 1-18 and seven more times in vv. 19-37

What Yahweh means: (a) the self-existing, ever-being God — 'I AM'; (b) God's covenantal name, communicating transcendency and relationship; (c) reveals all that is gloriously true of God (God is simple — all attributes inseparable)
The Aaronic blessing (Num 6:22-27): God puts his name upon his people as a blessing — Yahweh's name means relationship and blessing
Analogy: when you say your mother's name, everything you know about her floods your mind; on a far grander scale, this is what should happen when we say or read 'Yahweh'
Urging: become thoughtful readers of Scripture — ask why Yahweh is used instead of Adonai (or vice versa) in any given verse

outline.title: Point 2: The Application of the Commandments for Holy Living
subpoints: Observation 1: Obedience leads to holiness (vv. 3-4)
— v. 3: Honor (fear) father and mother — Fifth Commandment; holy living begins at home; Jesus rebuked Pharisees for Corban abuse (Mark 7:6-13); Paul reaffirmed in Eph 6:1-3
— v. 3: Keep the Sabbaths (plural) — Fourth Commandment; Sabbath is a creation ordinance; Jesus is Lord of the Sabbath and gave the Christian Sabbath on the first day of the week
— v. 4: Do not turn to idols — First Commandment; do not make molded gods — Second Commandment
Observation 2: Right worship leads to holiness (vv. 5-8)
— The peace offering is not to be profaned by being eaten on the third day
— We follow the regulative principle because adding to or taking from commanded worship profanes what God has made holy (Nadab and Abihu, Lev 10:1-3; Paul's rebuke of Corinthians, 1 Cor 11:20-22)
Observation 3: Love of neighbor leads to holiness (vv. 9-18)

— Refrain from greed; be generous (vv. 9-10): corners and gleaning set aside for the poor and stranger — application of 8th and 10th commandments; the story of Ruth; positive application: Eph 4:28; Luke 6:38
— Be honest in your dealings (vv. 11-12): application of 3rd, 8th, 9th, 10th commandments; let your yes be yes; Eph 4:29; Eph 5:4

— Be respectful (vv. 13-14): application of 6th, 8th, 9th, 10th commandments; do not curse the deaf or put a stumbling block before the blind — all image-bearers have dignity regardless of perceived quality of life
— Be just (vv. 15-16): application of 9th commandment; no partiality to poor or wealthy (James 2); guard your neighbor's reputation; do not be a false witness (Matt 26:59-60)

— Be loving (vv. 17-18): application of 6th commandment; love means confronting sin (1 Pet 4:8; James 5:19-20); not seeking revenge or bearing a grudge; the greatest commandment (Matt 22:34-40); Matthew 18:15 on reconciliation

outline.title: Conclusion
subpoints: We are not capable of full obedience to the Ten Commandments apart from the work of Christ and the Holy Spirit
Phil 2:12-13: we work out our sanctification with fear and trembling, because it is God who works in us the will and the do of his good pleasure
We are called to the same holiness as the Old Testament saints — the thread runs unbroken from Leviticus through Peter to us today

summary:
Elder Scott Hollander preaches on Leviticus 19:1-18 in a sermon that bridges the congregation's Westminster Larger Catechism Sunday School series on the Ten Commandments and Pastor Lyro's 1 Peter sermon series. The connection is explicit: 1 Peter 1:15-16 quotes Leviticus 19:2 directly — "Be holy, for I am holy." The sermon pursues three goals: to define holiness, to trace the justification for holy living, and to apply the Levitical commandments to the life of the modern church.

Holiness is defined as "utterly distinct, set apart" — Yahweh is transcendently set apart from all creatures because he alone is the uncreated, self-existing God. When he calls his people to be holy as he is holy, he calls them to share in his communicable attributes — righteousness, justice, love, mercy, grace, and kindness — and to be set apart from the surrounding nations. The justification for holy living is located in two foundations: the character of Yahweh himself, and the meaning of the covenant name Yahweh. Elder Hollander invests significant time in urging the congregation to become thoughtful readers of Scripture by attending to the distinction between Yahweh (God's personal, covenantal name, carrying all that is true of him in relationship to his people) and Adonai (Lord, a title of sovereignty), noting that the phrase "I am Yahweh your God" is repeated fourteen times in the chapter and that this repetition demands theological attention.

The application section moves through five observations: (1) obedience leads to holiness — beginning with honoring parents and keeping the Sabbath; (2) right worship leads to holiness — the peace offering passage illustrates the regulative principle of worship; (3) love of neighbor leads to holiness — traced through generosity to the poor, honesty in dealings, respect for all image-bearers regardless of their limitations, justice without partiality, and love that includes confronting sin and pursuing reconciliation. The sermon closes with an appeal to Phil 2:12-13: we work out our sanctification because it is God who works in us, and we cannot fulfill these commandments apart from the work of Christ and the Holy Spirit.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Leviticus 19:1-18

If you are able and willing to remain standing, please turn in your Bibles to Leviticus 19. We will read Leviticus 19, verses 1 through 18. Leviticus 19, starting in verse 1:

"And Yahweh spoke to Moses saying, 'Speak to all the congregation of the children of Israel, and say to them: You shall be holy, for I, Yahweh your God, am holy. Every one of you shall revere his mother and his father, and keep my Sabbaths. I am Yahweh your God. Do not turn to idols, nor make for yourselves molded gods. I am Yahweh your God. And if you offer a sacrifice of a peace offering to Yahweh, you shall offer it of your own free will. It shall be eaten the same day you offer it, and on the next day. And if any remains until the third day, it shall be burned in the fire. And if it is eaten at all on the third day, it is an abomination. It shall not be accepted. Therefore everyone who eats it shall bear his iniquity, because he has profaned the holy offering of Yahweh. And that person shall be cut off from his people. When you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not wholly reap the corners of your field, nor shall you gather the gleanings of your harvest. And you shall not glean your vineyard, nor shall you gather every grape of your vineyard. You shall leave them for the poor and the stranger. I am Yahweh your God. You shall not steal, nor deal falsely, nor lie to one another. And you shall not swear by my name falsely, nor shall you profane the name of your God. I am Yahweh. You shall not cheat your neighbor, nor rob him. The wages of him who has hired shall not remain with you all night until morning. You shall not curse the deaf, nor put a stumbling block before the blind, but shall fear your God. I am Yahweh. You shall do no injustice in judgment. You shall not be partial to the poor, nor honor the person of the mighty. In righteousness you shall judge your neighbor. You shall not go about as a talebearer among your people. Nor shall you take a stand against the life of your neighbor. I am Yahweh. You shall not hate your brother in your heart. You shall surely rebuke your neighbor, and not bear sin because of him. You shall not take vengeance, nor bear any grudge against the children of your people, but you shall love your neighbor as yourself. I am Yahweh.'"

May the Lord bless the reading of his word. You may be seated.

## Introduction: Why Leviticus 19?

Our Heavenly Father, we do ask that you would bless the reading of your word. May I, your servant, not be a distraction to your people this morning, but may your word go forth boldly. Keep my words from error. Keep my tongue from stumbling. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.

Perhaps you are wondering why I chose to preach on Leviticus 19 for a sermon this morning. If you know me, maybe you are not surprised at all — I do enjoy studying the Old Testament, and that is certainly part of it. I have no Hebrew words for you today, except for two: Yahweh and Adonai. Those are the only two Hebrew words you may hear me say this morning. I was very conscientious about that — I moved any others I had in my notes to the footnotes so I would not accidentally say them.

I have created a couple of charts to help keep some of this straight. Since we follow the regulative principle and do not use PowerPoint slides during the service, I have printed PowerPoint slides.

Why Leviticus 19? I thought it fit nicely into our Sunday school series, as we have been considering the Ten Commandments as expressed and explained through the Larger Catechism. As I recall, we have made it through the eighth commandment. In November we will close out the series with the ninth and tenth commandments and a follow-on lesson.

The other providentially convenient reason came from Pastor Lyro's sermon series in 1 Peter. It is there where Peter quotes from the Greek translation of Leviticus 19 when he writes, "But as he who called you is holy, you also be holy in all your conduct, because it is written, 'Be holy, for I am holy.'" I hope you remember that from his sermons of a few weeks ago.

Hopefully you will notice the repeating threads from both the Sunday school series and Pastor Lyro's sermons from 1 Peter as we consider Leviticus 19:1-18 this morning.

## Defining Holiness

One commentator wrote: "'You must be holy for I, the Lord your God, am holy' could be described as the motto of Leviticus." This phrase first appears in Leviticus 11:44-45, is repeated in chapter 19 as we heard read this morning, and is repeated again in Leviticus 20:7 and 26.

Before we get into the meat of the text, I want to define our key word: holy. What does God mean when he says that he is holy and we are to be holy? Holy is a complex term that can be described as meaning utterly distinct, set apart. Yahweh is utterly distinct because he is the sole creator. There is no other person or being that existed before creation. Only Yahweh is everlastingly existent. The spirit world contains created beings — angels and demons — that are far more powerful than a human. Yet all other powerful spirit beings are all within the creation order. Yahweh stands transcendently set apart from any so-called deity and mankind because Yahweh created us all.

When Yahweh calls for his people to be holy since he is holy, he wants us to be set apart from the nations and the people around us. Paul reminds us in Ephesians 1:4 that we were chosen in Christ before the foundation of the world to be holy and blameless. And in Ephesians 2:10 he reiterates that we were prepared beforehand to walk in good works.

Yahweh calls us to be holy as he is holy — meaning everything within his communicable attributes that reflect his holiness is to be shared by his people. The communicable attributes — those that created beings are capable of sharing with their creator — include righteousness, justice, love, mercy, grace, and kindness. Think of the fruit of the Spirit from Galatians 5:22-23: love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control. These attributes are to describe us because they are part of how we can grasp the holiness of God.

## Point 1: The Justification for Holy Living

### Justification 1: Yahweh Is Holy (vv. 1-2)

"And Yahweh spoke to Moses saying, 'Speak to all the congregation of the children of Israel, and say to them: You shall be holy, for Yahweh your God am holy.'" The word *for* denotes reason. That is the justification for God's command to his people: God demands holiness from his people because he is holy. He expects his people to be a reflection of his holiness.

This is emphasized in two ways. First, Yahweh declares that his character is holy: "I, Yahweh, am holy." Second, Yahweh reminds his people that he is their God: "You shall be holy for Yahweh *your* God am holy."

This message is for the modern church as well. Jesus taught the same principle in the Sermon on the Mount: "Therefore you shall be perfect, just as your Father in heaven is perfect" (Matt 5:48). And Peter applied the Levitical command of holiness to the New Testament church: "But as he who called you is holy, you also be holy in all your conduct, because it is written, 'Be holy, for I am holy.'" (1 Pet 1:15-16).

### Justification 2: The Name Yahweh

Here I need to spend some time so that we can reflect as thoughtful readers on what the statement "I am Yahweh your God" actually means. There is a weightiness to this statement that I do not want you to miss.

First, observe the pattern in which this phrase is repeated with each application of the commandments: God commands, "obey — because I am Yahweh your God; live this way — because I am Yahweh." This phrase is repeated seven times in verses 1 through 18, and another seven times in verses 19 through 37.

As I stated earlier, we need to note what God said in contrast to what he did not say. He was not merely saying that since he is the Creator and we are the creation, we must obey. Nor was he merely stating that since he is the King of the universe — our sovereign Lord — and we are his subjects, we must obey. God could have said these things. He is the creator and sustainer. He is our King. He is our sovereign Lord. We owe him our fealty, our allegiance, our obedience. But what God did say was not to stress his titles in this proclamation, but to stress his *name*, Yahweh.

We must thoughtfully ask ourselves: why?

First, what does Yahweh mean? He is the self-existing, ever-being God. He told Moses, "I AM." And then God told Moses, "You may call me 'He is.'" God is everlasting — he always was, always is, and always will be. When we say his name, we acknowledge: he is.

Second, Yahweh is God's covenantal name to his people. The name communicates both transcendency and relationship — the grandness of God and his intimate relationship with his people. His name was so important in worship and relationship that God instructed that his name be *applied to* the people for their blessing — placed upon them. The Aaronic blessing in Numbers 6:22-27: "Yahweh will bless you and keep you. Yahweh will make his face shine upon you and be gracious to you. Yahweh will lift up his face upon you and give you peace. And they will put my name on the Israelites, and I will bless them." It is through the covenantal name of Yahweh that his people experience relationship and blessing.

Third, the name reveals all that is gloriously true of God. Remember that God is *simple* — none of his attributes, none of what defines the goodness of God, can be separated from the others. When we have and understand the name of God, it rings all that is true of him. One commentator states: "God's name stands for himself and all that is gloriously true of him." Another: "The name stands in Holy Scripture for the nature or revealed character of God, and not as a mere label or title." A third: "To know God's name is to know him and his character."

**A practical exercise:** Members of our congregation, your session knows you, we love you, we care about you, we pray for you. In every session meeting, we move alphabetically through the list of members and select ten families or member names, and we pray for you. We say, "How is this person serving in the church? How might we pray for her? How might we help him in his walk with Christ?" What we are acknowledging when we think of you and your name is everything we know about you. Now, on a far grander scale, this is what should be happening when we say the name of Yahweh. I urge you: become more thoughtful readers of Scripture. When you see "Yahweh" (or "LORD" in most English translations), ask yourself: what are the implications of Scripture's use of Yahweh in this verse? Why did the author use Adonai (Lord) here instead? This extra mental energy in reading and meditating on the passage will bless you and foster growth as you discuss these questions with family members and friends at church.

## Point 2: The Application of the Commandments for Holy Living

### Observation 1: Obedience Leads to Holiness (vv. 3-4)

In verse 3, the first command is to revere father and mother — the Fifth Commandment. Holy living begins at home. The verb here is *fear* — children are called to fear their parents in a similar way we are called to fear God. Perhaps by beginning with this command first, God was instructing Moses and the community that holy living begins at home.

Jesus emphatically reiterated this in the New Testament. In Mark 7:6-13, he called the Pharisees hypocrites and accused them of laying aside the commandment of God for the tradition of men — specifically, the Corban abuse, by which they could set aside money for God and thereby excuse themselves from supporting their aging parents. Paul also reminded the Ephesian church: "Children, obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right. Honor your father and mother, which is the first commandment with promise, that it may be well with you and you may live long on the earth" (Eph 6:1-3).

Just as honoring parents is of prime importance in our human duties, keeping the Sabbath is our primary spiritual duty. "Keep the Sabbaths" — note that Sabbaths is plural, perhaps referring to all of the Sabbaths commanded to Israel (every seventh day, plus Pentecost, the Day of Atonement, and so on). The fourth commandment in Exodus 20 is the longest of the commandments, and the Sabbath is a creation ordinance — established before the fall. Jesus validated that he is Lord of the Sabbath, and having rightly observed the Sabbath during his life, he gave his followers the Christian Sabbath to worship and rest on the first day of the week — still in the pattern of one in seven, on the Lord's Day.

In verse 4: "Do not turn to idols" — First Commandment. "Nor make for yourselves molded gods" — Second Commandment. These are the prohibitions of false worship.

### Observation 2: Right Worship Leads to Holiness (vv. 5-8)

Here Moses records God's instruction about the peace offering — a sacrifice in which both the priest and the individual share in the feast. What we observe is punishment for making common or profane what God has set apart as holy. If any of the peace offering is eaten on the third day, it is an abomination.

Holy living means worshiping in the manner that God has established. This is why we follow the regulative principle of worship: we worship only in the manner that God has prescribed in his word. By adding to or taking away from the commanded means of worship, the church and the individual risk profaning that which God has made holy.

Do you remember from Leviticus 10 the account of Nadab and Abihu, who brought a profane fire before the Lord in worship? God killed them on the spot. Fire proceeded from Yahweh and consumed them. And Moses said to Aaron: "This is what Yahweh spoke, saying, 'By those who come near me I must be regarded as holy; and before all the people I must be glorified.'" Likewise, Paul had to rebuke the Corinthian church for profaning how they partook of the Lord's Supper (1 Cor 11:20-22). This is why we take how we worship, what we include in worship, and our behavior in worship very seriously.

### Observation 3: Love of Neighbor Leads to Holiness (vv. 9-18)

**First, refrain from greed; be generous (vv. 9-10)** — application of the Eighth and Tenth Commandments. God designated the corners and edges of the field, and the gleaning (the grain dropped in harvesting), to be set aside for the poor and the stranger. To take all of the harvest — gathering the edges and every grape — is theft because that portion does not belong to the owner of the field. Note the promise of the Fifth Commandment (in Exodus 20:12) is tied to the land given to them.

You might recall the story of Ruth. As widows and destitute, Ruth and Naomi were permitted — and even expected — to glean in the local fields. God apportioned the edges and the gleanings for the poor as an act of generosity, but the poor were still obliged to work. This was not a free handout for the lazy — it is a mercy that the poor may come and work rather than be embarrassed by asking for charity.

The Christian is further called to generosity — not only prohibited from stealing but compelled to give. Ephesians 4:28: "Let him who stole steal no longer, but rather let him labor, working with his hands what is good, that he may have something to give him who has need." Luke 6:38: "Give, and it will be given to you. Good measure, pressed down, shaken together, running over... for with the measure you use it will be measured back to you."

**Second, be honest in your dealings (vv. 11-12)** — application of the Third, Eighth, Ninth, and Tenth Commandments. In the Sermon on the Mount, Christ taught that his disciples were not to swear falsely; rather they would fulfill their oaths and vows. Matthew 5 and 23 say that God's people are to be known for their honesty. James said: let your yes be yes and your no, no (James 5:12). Paul in Ephesians 4:29: "Let no corrupt word proceed from your mouth, but only what is necessary for edification."

**Third, be respectful (vv. 13-14)** — application of the Sixth, Eighth, Ninth, and Tenth Commandments. "You shall not curse the deaf, nor put a stumbling block before the blind." These verses instruct us in the sanctity of life regardless of its perceived quality — poor, deaf, blind, handicapped, mentally challenged, still in the womb: all are made in the image of God. To harm any human being is to dishonor the image of God. The root word translated as curse means to make slight, to belittle. Any mocking, cursing, or belittling of a weaker person fails to respect the person made in the image of God. One commentator put it well: "God looks after those who are disadvantaged, even though the deaf may not be aware of those who curse them, nor the blind of those who put obstacles in their way. One who fears God will not mistreat the disadvantaged."

**Fourth, be just (vv. 15-16)** — application of the Ninth Commandment. "You shall do no injustice in judgment. You shall not be partial to the poor, nor honor the person of the mighty." No favoritism — to the poor or to the wealthy. James 2 reiterates the principles of impartiality over thirteen verses. We are called to be just in our dealings, to show no partiality, and to guard our neighbor's reputation — not go about as a talebearer, not bear false witness. Matthew 26:59-60 records the priests seeking false witnesses against Jesus at his trial — the ultimate context for understanding what false witness can do.

**Fifth, be loving (vv. 17-18)** — application of the Sixth Commandment. "You shall not hate your brother in your heart. You shall surely rebuke your neighbor, and not bear sin because of him. You shall not take vengeance, nor bear any grudge against the children of your people, but you shall love your neighbor as yourself. I am Yahweh."

Note that showing love to your neighbor means confronting sin. 1 Peter 4:8: love covers a multitude of sin — but that covering includes the process of repentance, not merely glossing over sin. James 5:19-20 reminds us that we are to call the sinner from his sin. Matthew 22:34-40: to love our neighbor is one of the greatest commandments. And Matthew 18:15: we are called as Christians to confront sin and to seek reconciliation through repentance.

Loving your neighbor further means that we are not to seek revenge or bear a grudge. Christians are called to reconciliation. This is the first part of church discipline — confronting sin to seek repentance and restoration.

## Conclusion

I know the second half of the sermon was somewhat hasty, with a lot of complicated application. But I trust that you can walk away acknowledging how this passage gives justification for holy living, and that it has spurred you to become a more thoughtful reader of Scripture as you encounter the names and titles of God in your reading.

Believers today are called to holy living in the same manner that the saints of the Old Testament church in the nation of Israel were called to holiness. It is critical to remember, though, that we are not capable of fulfilling full obedience to the Ten Commandments apart from the work of Christ and the Holy Spirit in our lives. As you consider today your calling to holy living, I also hope you will be empowered to obedience. Paul reminds us in Philippians 2:12-13 that we work out our sanctification with fear and trembling, because it is God who works in us the will and the do of his good pleasure.

May God be blessed in the reading and application of his word for us today.

## Closing Prayer

Our Heavenly Father, we do ask that you would bless your word. Let the richness of your word be translated to your people. May they forget my words, my rambling tongue, and may they focus on what your word has for your people — that they would be empowered by your Holy Spirit to live a life of holiness as you have called us to. We ask this in the name of your Son, Jesus Christ. Amen.
